title: ACAS Code of Practice 5: Handling in a Reasonable Manner Requests to Work Flexibly
topicLabel: Managing flexible working requests from employees
issuedUnder: Trade Union and Labour Relations (Consolidation) Act 1992 s.199
replacedPrevious: Previous ACAS guidance on flexible working (non-statutory). This is the first statutory Code of Practice on flexible working.
chapters: Statutory Code of Practice · Issued under TULRCA 1992 s.199 · Laid before Parliament December 2023 · In effect 6 April 2024 · Accompanies the Employment Relations (Flexible Working) Act 2023 reforms
desc: The ACAS Code of Practice on Handling in a Reasonable Manner Requests to Work Flexibly was introduced alongside the Employment Relations (Flexible Working) Act 2023 reforms which took effect on 6 April 2024. The reforms removed the 26-week qualifying period for flexible working requests (making it a day-one right), allowed employees to make two requests in any 12-month period (previously one), reduced the employer's decision period from three months to two months, and removed the requirement for employees to explain the effect of the requested change. The Code sets out the standards of reasonable behaviour expected of employers and employees when handling statutory flexible working requests under ERA 1996 ss.80F–80I. Employment tribunals may adjust awards by up to 25% for unreasonable failure to comply with the Code.
legalStatus: Statutory Code of Practice. Tribunals will take the Code into account. Awards may be adjusted by up to 25% for unreasonable non-compliance (TULRCA 1992 s.207A).

parts.title: The Statutory Right to Request Flexible Working

sections.title: Right to request, eligibility, and types of flexibility
sections.summary: Since 6 April 2024, all employees have a day-one right to make a statutory request for flexible working under ERA 1996 s.80F. Employees may make up to two statutory requests in any 12-month period. Flexible working can include changes to hours (e.g. part-time, compressed hours, staggered hours, annualised hours), changes to times of work (e.g. flexitime, shift changes), changes to place of work (e.g. home working, hybrid working), and job-sharing. The request must be in writing, state it is a statutory request under ERA 1996 s.80F, specify the change sought and the proposed start date, and state whether and when a previous statutory request was made. The employee is no longer required to explain the effect of the change on the employer.
sections.text:
From 6 April 2024, all employees have the right from the first day of employment to make a statutory request for flexible working. An employee may make up to two statutory requests in any 12-month period.

A statutory request must be in writing, state that it is made under section 80F of the Employment Rights Act 1996, specify the change sought and the proposed start date, and state whether and when a previous statutory request was made.

Flexible working can include:
• changes to the hours an employee works (e.g. part-time, compressed hours, staggered hours, annualised hours)
• changes to the times an employee works (e.g. flexitime, shift swapping, different start or finish times)
• changes to the place an employee works (e.g. working from home, hybrid working)
• job sharing

The employer must deal with the request in a reasonable manner and notify the employee of the decision within the decision period (two months from the date of the request, unless the parties agree to extend).

versions.description: Original right to request flexible working introduced by Employment Act 2002. Limited to parents of children under 6 (or under 18 if disabled). Required 26 weeks' continuous employment.

Children and Families Act 2014 extended the right to all employees with 26 weeks' continuous employment. Removed the limitation to parents/carers. Replaced the statutory procedure with a duty to deal with requests in a reasonable manner.

Employment Relations (Flexible Working) Act 2023 reforms: day-one right (26-week qualifying period removed); two requests per 12 months (was one); decision period reduced from three months to two months; requirement to explain effect on employer removed; employer must consult before refusing.
versions.policyRationale: The reforms were introduced as part of the government's response to changing working patterns, particularly the shift to hybrid and remote working accelerated by the COVID-19 pandemic. The objective was to make flexible working the default rather than the exception.

leadingCases.name: Commotion Ltd v Rutty [2006] ICR 290
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that the right to request flexible working is a right to have the request seriously considered, not a right to have the request granted. The employer must follow the statutory procedure and can only refuse on one of the prescribed business grounds.
leadingCases.significance: Established that the right is procedural (serious consideration) not substantive (right to flexible working)

refs: ERA 1996 ss.80F–80I (statutory right to request flexible working)
ERA 1996 s.80G (employer's duties in relation to application)
ERA 1996 s.80H (grounds for refusal — 8 prescribed business grounds)

sections.title: Reasonable handling: meeting, consultation, and decision
sections.summary: The employer must deal with the request in a reasonable manner. The Code establishes that reasonable handling includes: discussing the request with the employee to understand what they are seeking and why, considering alternatives if the employer cannot accommodate the specific request, responding without unreasonable delay, explaining clearly in writing the reasons for any refusal, and only refusing on one of the eight prescribed business grounds in ERA 1996 s.80H. From 6 April 2024, the employer must consult with the employee before refusing a request. The employer is not required to hold a formal meeting but should discuss the request with the employee unless the employer intends to approve it without modification.
sections.text:
The employer should:
• discuss the request with the employee to understand what they are seeking and explore whether and how the request might be accommodated
• consider the request carefully, looking at the benefits of the requested changes and weighing these against any adverse business impact
• consider whether a trial period or alternative arrangement might be appropriate if the employer is unsure whether the request can be accommodated
• consult with the employee before refusing the request (mandatory from 6 April 2024)
• make a decision within two months of the request being made, unless the parties agree to extend
• communicate the decision in writing

If refusing the request, the employer must state the business ground(s) for refusal and explain why the ground(s) apply in the circumstances. The eight prescribed business grounds under ERA 1996 s.80H are:
(a) the burden of additional costs
(b) detrimental effect on ability to meet customer demand
(c) inability to reorganise work among existing staff
(d) inability to recruit additional staff
(e) detrimental impact on quality
(f) detrimental impact on performance
(g) insufficiency of work during the periods the employee proposes to work
(h) planned structural changes

leadingCases.name: Hardy v Tourism South East [2005] UKEAT/0396/04
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT considered the standard of reasoning required when an employer refuses a flexible working request. The employer must provide a genuine and considered explanation linked to the statutory business grounds. A formulaic or generic refusal is insufficient.
leadingCases.significance: Established that genuine, case-specific reasoning is required when refusing flexible working requests

refs: ERA 1996 s.80G (employer's duties — requirement to deal with request in reasonable manner)
ERA 1996 s.80H (prescribed business grounds for refusal)
ERA 1996 s.80I (complaints to employment tribunal — up to 8 weeks' pay)
EqA 2010 s.19 (indirect discrimination — flexible working refusals may constitute indirect sex/disability discrimination)

sections.title: Appeals and indirect discrimination considerations
sections.summary: The Code recommends that employers provide an appeal process for refused flexible working requests, although there is no statutory right of appeal. Employers should be aware that blanket refusals of flexible working requests, or policies that disproportionately affect employees with protected characteristics (particularly women with childcare responsibilities, or disabled employees), may constitute indirect discrimination under the Equality Act 2010. The employer's obligation to make reasonable adjustments under EqA 2010 s.20 may also require flexible working arrangements for disabled employees, independently of the statutory request procedure.
sections.text:
The Code recommends that where a request is refused, the employer should offer the employee the right to appeal. While there is no statutory right of appeal in the flexible working request procedure, providing one is good practice and may help resolve the issue without the need for a tribunal claim.

Employers should be aware that a refusal of a flexible working request could give rise to a claim of indirect discrimination under the Equality Act 2010, particularly where the requirement to work in a particular way disproportionately affects employees who share a protected characteristic. For example, a requirement to work full-time may disproportionately affect women with childcare responsibilities.

Separately, disabled employees may be entitled to flexible working as a reasonable adjustment under section 20 of the Equality Act 2010, regardless of whether they have made a statutory request.

leadingCases.name: Dobson v North Cumbria Integrated Care NHS Foundation Trust [2021] UKEAT/0220/19
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that a requirement to work full-time can constitute a provision, criterion or practice (PCP) that puts women at a particular disadvantage for the purposes of indirect sex discrimination. The employer must justify the requirement as a proportionate means of achieving a legitimate aim.
leadingCases.significance: Recent authority confirming that full-time working requirements can constitute indirect sex discrimination

leadingCases.name: Chief Constable of Lincolnshire Police v Caston [2009] EWCA Civ 1298
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal confirmed that part-time working requirements or refusals of flexible working can constitute indirect sex discrimination where they disproportionately affect women. The employer must justify the refusal.
leadingCases.significance: Court of Appeal authority on the intersection of flexible working refusals and indirect sex discrimination

refs: EqA 2010 s.20 (duty to make reasonable adjustments for disabled employees)
EqA 2010 s.13 (direct discrimination — if refusal is because of a protected characteristic)

pendingAmendments: ERA 2025 — further strengthening of flexible working framework (not yet in force)
Code 5 in effect from 6 April 2024 alongside EReFW Act 2023 reforms

parliamentaryOrigin.issuedUnder: Trade Union and Labour Relations (Consolidation) Act 1992 s.199
parliamentaryOrigin.statutoryBasis: Statutory Code of Practice — approved by resolution of both Houses of Parliament
parliamentaryOrigin.legalEffect: Employment tribunals must take the Code into account. s.207A 25% adjustment applies. Code came into effect alongside the Employment Relations (Flexible Working) Act 2023 reforms: day-one right, two requests per year, 2-month decision period, consultation before refusal.
parliamentaryOrigin.note: Code of Practice 5 is a new statutory code — the first ACAS Code specifically on flexible working. It replaces previous non-statutory ACAS guidance. Its introduction alongside the EReFW Act 2023 reforms means flexible working non-compliance now carries the s.207A 25% adjustment risk for the first time.

precedingDocuments.title: Employment Relations (Flexible Working) Act 2023
precedingDocuments.significance: Transformed the flexible working framework: day-one right, two requests per year, 2-month decision period. The ACAS Code was issued alongside these reforms.

leadingCases.name: Commotion Ltd v Rutty [2006] ICR 290
leadingCases.heldText: The right to request flexible working is procedural — serious consideration, not guaranteed outcome.

leadingCases.name: Dobson v North Cumbria Integrated Care NHS Foundation Trust [2021] UKEAT/0220/19
leadingCases.heldText: Flexible working refusal can constitute indirect sex discrimination where the requirement to work full-time disproportionately disadvantages women.
leadingCases.significance: Links flexible working refusals to indirect discrimination.
